# UC-10.13.47 · Registry Modification Tracking

## Description

Persistence via `Registry` keys — `action=set` on Run keys.

## Value

Persistence via `Registry` keys — `action=set` on Run keys.

## Implementation

Allowlist IT deployment tools; alert on `process_name` = unusual parents.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Sysmon → Endpoint.Registry.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `registry_path`, `process_name`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Allowlist IT deployment tools; alert on `process_name` = unusual parents.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=security tag=endpoint registry_path="*\\CurrentVersion\\Run*"
| stats count by host, registry_path, process_name
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Registry Modification Tracking** — Persistence via `Registry` keys — `action=set` on Run keys.

Documented **Data sources**: `registry_path`, `process_name`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Sysmon → Endpoint.Registry. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: security.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=security, tags. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, registry_path, process_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count from datamodel=Endpoint.Registry where match(Registry.registry_path,"CurrentVersion\\\\Run") by Registry.dest, Registry.registry_path, Registry.process_name
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Registry Modification Tracking** — Persistence via `Registry` keys — `action=set` on Run keys.

Documented **Data sources**: `registry_path`, `process_name`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Sysmon → Endpoint.Registry. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Endpoint.Registry` — enable acceleration for that model.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Accelerate the CIM data model used in the optional tstats path, then run both the primary and `cimSpl` searches over a week that includes a known good baseline. Spot-check a few rows and tune thresholds; confirm no permission or tagging gap is hiding part of the population.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (modifications), Sunburst (path).

## SPL

```spl
index=security tag=endpoint registry_path="*\\CurrentVersion\\Run*"
| stats count by host, registry_path, process_name
| sort -count
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count from datamodel=Endpoint.Registry where match(Registry.registry_path,"CurrentVersion\\\\Run") by Registry.dest, Registry.registry_path, Registry.process_name
```

## Visualization

Table (modifications), Sunburst (path).
